exercises.example_sentence: 宿題はもう終わりました。
wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still / not yet,' so 'まだ終わりました' contradicts itself — 'still' suggests the homework isn't done, but the verb says it is.
やっと means 'finally / at long last,' which would add a sense of struggle or relief. The sentence is a simple, neutral report of completion, not a comment on how hard it was.
また means 'again,' so 'また終わりました' would say the homework got finished a second time. The sentence is reporting a first-time completion, not a repeat.
exercises.hiragana_full: しゅくだいはもうおわりました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 宿題はもう全部やりました。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: しゅくだいはもうぜんぶやりました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 宿題はさっき終わったところです。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: しゅくだいはさっきおわったところです。

exercises.example_sentence: コーヒーをもう一杯ください。
exercises.translation: One more cup of coffee, please.
wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ一杯' would oddly say 'still one cup,' as if commenting on a remaining amount. The speaker is requesting an additional cup, not noting what's left.
ぜんぶ means 'all,' which clashes with the counter 一杯 (one cup). The sentence asks for one extra cup, not the whole pot.
ちょっと means 'a little,' so 'ちょっと一杯' awkwardly mixes 'a bit' with the specific 'one cup' count. The speaker wants one more full cup, not a small portion.
exercises.hiragana_full: こーひーをもういっぱいください。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もう一杯コーヒーをいただけますか。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もういっぱいこーひーをいただけますか。

audio_alternatives.kanji: コーヒーのおかわりをお願いします。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: こーひーのおかわりをおねがいします。

wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ出ました' contradicts itself — 'still' implies the train hasn't departed, but 出ました says it did.
ずっと means 'continuously / the whole time,' which doesn't fit 出る — a one-time departure isn't an ongoing action that can stretch across time.
ちょうど means 'just / exactly now,' so 'ちょうど出ました' pins the departure to this very second. The sentence emphasizes that the train is already gone, not the precise moment it left.
exercises.hiragana_full: でんしゃはもうでました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 電車はもう発車しました。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: でんしゃはもうはっしゃしました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 次の電車を待たないといけません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: つぎのでんしゃをまたないといけません。

exercises.translation: My little sister is already a middle schooler.
wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ中学生' would frame middle school as a stage she hasn't moved past — flipping the 'grown up so fast' nuance the sentence carries.
やっと means 'finally,' so 'やっと中学生' would say she reached middle school after a long wait. The sentence expresses surprise at how soon it happened, not relief after a delay.
ちょうど means 'exactly,' so 'ちょうど中学生' would just precisely identify her grade. The sentence does more than that — it conveys that she's already this old.
exercises.hiragana_full: いもうとはもうちゅうがくせいです。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 妹はこの春から中学生になりました。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: いもうとはこのはるからちゅうがくせいになりました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もう妹も中学生か、早いね。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もういもうともちゅうがくせいか、はやいね。

wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still / not yet,' so 'まだすぐ' doesn't form a natural phrase for 'soon' — the sentence is about something close at hand, not something delayed.
また means 'again,' so 'またすぐ' would imply spring just left and is coming back around. The sentence is about the upcoming arrival, not a quick return.
やっと means 'finally,' so 'やっとすぐ' is awkward — やっと implies a long wait is now over, while the sentence simply forecasts that spring is near.
exercises.hiragana_full: もうすぐはるがきます。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 春はもうすぐそこまで来ています。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: はるはもうすぐそこまできています。

audio_alternatives.kanji: あと少しで春が始まります。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あとすこしではるがはじまります。

exercises.example_sentence: お腹がいっぱいなので、もう食べられません。
exercises.translation: I'm full, so I can't eat anymore.
wrong_choice_explanations: まだ with a negative means 'not yet,' so 'まだ食べられません' would say 'I can't eat yet,' implying the food isn't ready. The sentence is about being too full to continue, not about timing.
ぜんぜん means 'not at all,' so 'ぜんぜん食べられません' would say no eating is possible from the start. The lead-in 'お腹がいっぱい' makes it clear the speaker has been eating — they just can't add more.
あまり with a negative means 'not very much,' so 'あまり食べられません' would soften this to 'I can't eat much.' The sentence is stronger — they can't take another bite at all.
exercises.hiragana_full: おなかがいっぱいなので、もうたべられません。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もうこれ以上は食べられません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もうこれいじょうはたべられません。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 満腹で、これ以上は入りません。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: まんぷくで、これいじょうははいりません。

wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ寝る時間' would say the bedtime window hasn't closed yet — implying you don't have to go to bed now. The sentence is doing the opposite: announcing it's time.
また means 'again,' so 'また寝る時間' would say bedtime has rolled around once more. The sentence isn't comparing to past nights; it's signaling that tonight's bedtime has arrived.
ずっと means 'the whole time,' so 'ずっと寝る時間' would oddly say it has been bedtime continuously. The sentence is about a single point — right now is the time to sleep.
exercises.hiragana_full: もうねるじかんですよ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: そろそろ寝る時間ですよ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: そろそろねるじかんですよ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もう遅いから、早く寝なさい。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もうおそいから、はやくねなさい。

exercises.example_sentence: 父はもう家に帰りました。
exercises.translation: My dad has already come home.
wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ帰りました' contradicts itself — 'still' suggests he hasn't come home, but 帰りました says he has.
ちょうど means 'just / exactly,' so 'ちょうど帰りました' pinpoints the moment of arrival ('he just got home'). The sentence emphasizes that he's already back, not the precise minute he walked in.
ずっと means 'the whole time,' which doesn't combine naturally with 帰る — 帰る marks a single arrival, not an ongoing state stretching through time.
exercises.hiragana_full: ちちはもういえにかえりました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 父はもう帰宅しています。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ちちはもうきたくしています。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 今、父はリビングでテレビを見ています。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: いま、ちちはりびんぐでてれびをみています。

wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ止みました' contradicts itself — 'still' suggests the rain is ongoing, but 止みました says it has ended.
ずっと means 'the whole time,' which doesn't combine with 止む — stopping is a single transition, not a continuous action stretched across time.
また means 'again,' so 'また止みました' would say the rain has stopped a second time. The sentence is about one rainfall that has now ended, not a recurring pattern.
exercises.hiragana_full: あめはもうやみました。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 雨はもう上がりましたよ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あめはもうあがりましたよ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 外を見ると、雨はすっかりやんでいました。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: そとをみると、あめはすっかりやんでいました。

exercises.example_sentence: すみません、もう一度言ってください。
exercises.translation: Sorry, please say that one more time.
wrong_choice_explanations: まだ means 'still,' so 'まだ一度' would oddly say 'still one time,' which doesn't form a natural request. The speaker is asking for one additional repetition.
また means 'again' on its own, so 'また一度言ってください' is redundant — また 一度 doesn't form the standard 'one more time' phrase. The fixed expression is もう一度.
ぜんぶ means 'all,' so 'ぜんぶ一度言ってください' would awkwardly request 'all of it in one go.' The speaker just wants the previous statement repeated, not crammed together.
exercises.hiragana_full: すみません、もういちどいってください。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もう一回お願いできますか。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もういっかいおねがいできますか。

audio_alternatives.kanji: すみません、もう一度繰り返してもらえますか。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: すみません、もういちどくりかえしてもらえますか。
